item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kept getting me off guard. Nice work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Isn't this supposed to be Neutralized?
Or is Foundation expecting same things to happen in a short time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the object, and I like the vague little not-an-explanation-at-all that the unnamed person of interest provides in the interview log. There are some confusing bits, though.

The places where black boxes are used in place of numbers (e.g. "DNA from over ██████ individuals") are ambiguous- the length of the boxes makes it unclear whether they're covering very large numbers written with digits ("DNA from over 100000 individuals") or smaller numbers written in words ("DNA from over twenty individuals"). Based on the use of black boxes with numbers elsewhere in the article (e.g. "date of ██/██/2007") I'm inclined to think they're digits… but I feel like a mass suicide in the hundreds of thousands would have been worldwide news, especially if the Foundation had no reason to cover it up at the time.

Also, the Foundation seems to be behaving oddly toward the end. Why does Hamlin never ask what Greenflight was a subdivision of? If Greenflight was only one part of something larger, and Greenflight made 2198, I would think the Foundation would want to investigate the hell out of that larger thing. Am I missing something obvious here?

If the Foundation hasn't found any other Greenflight documentation, is the surviving population a group they've identified from DNA analysis, or is that fact purely inferred from the unnamed participant's testimony? Either way, how are they planning on contacting these people from (at best) samples of their DNA?

If there are still Greenflight survivors out there, and that's enough evidence to keep the phenomenon listed as Euclid and task satellites just in case it shows up again, why has the Foundation stopped trying to figure out what Greenflight was?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
geosynchronous geostationary orbit

The geostationary orbit is the special case of a geosynchronous orbit in the plane of the equator. Geosynchronous orbits are possible at any inclination provided the satellite is traveling at a distance of ~42,000 km from the mass center of the earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't have a truly geosynchronous orbit anywhere but over the equator. You can have it so that it will always stay over the same longitude but the latitude will shift through out the day. so perhaps a few satalites that are arranged so that at least one of them always has a line of sight to area in question would be more realistic for this purpose

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, no, I think you're absolutely right. At least one guy involved in their creation is still running around, who's to say more aren't and that they can't do it again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, I've removed the addendum note.

As much as I'd love to have a Neutralised article, the Foundation can't be sure that the clouds aren't going to come back, and are therefore unsure as to the nature of it. I was considering Euclid/Neutralised, but I thought that that wouldn't really go down well, what with the whole "frowned-upon" mentality regarding double object classes and all. If enough people think it's justified to be Neutralised or Euclid/Neutralised, then I'll change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The twist at the very end is good, but the objects themselves are pretty heavy-handed as far as the idea goes, and the note with the collapsible is really poor. You should probably just take that out. Also, shouldn't this be Neutralized?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of nitpicky, but an honest question: Can you have a geosynchronous orbit that far south? I'm not sure and wasn't really able to find an answer after a (cursory) search. If it is, then disregard.

Otherwise, I'm not particularly blown away but this isn't bad, so I'm sitting at neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. I still do have a few issues with it, but it's definitely deserving of an up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember seeing the first draft of this, without the final conversation log, and I was doubtful it would work out.

I'm glad I was wrong. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I know it's something that sounds like it's been directly pulled from a crime drama, but I couldnt really think of any other way that the Foundation would confront somebody for lying other than by being blunt. I'll wait to see if anyone else finds this as annoying, and then I'll try to come up with ways to change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just felt it worded in a way to feel kinda unrealistic. Could just be me, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback. What makes the lying part awkward?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I saw "clouds made of tears," I immediately sighed and predicted I'd downvote based on cliché. The saliva bit caught me off guard, though, and I liked it enough by the end to upvote. Honestly, I'm not sure this is great, but it definitely intrigued me. However, I do think the "I'm not lying to you" bit is super-awkward, and could do with some editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I had assumed so, seeing as the code worked in my sandbox, and it wasn't any different to what I had put in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just copy and pasted the code, and it worked. I recall something like the image not appearing after uploading an article happening before, so it was probably just on your end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having trouble with the image. The code is here, and the file attached to the page has the same name. I've removed the code for the time being.

[[include component:image-block name=2395541237_0c8533183a_o.jpg|caption=SCP-2198, as unintentionally photographed by photographer ████ ███████-████████ on ██/██/2007. SCP-2198, visible on the left, has undergone predominant dissipation.]]

Other than this, thanks to Adam Grey for the critique. Here's hoping that this is a lot better than what was posted in the forums.

Edit: The image that I'm trying to use is from here:

https://www.flickr.com/photos/coastguardnews/2395541237/
